content.full_text:
RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
February 27,
1996
Martha .Clark Lofgren
City Attorney
City of Folsom
Office of the City Attorney
50 Natoma Street
Folsoin, California
95630
Re:
Your Request for Advice
•
Our File No. A-96-042
Dear Ms.
Lofgren:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
City of Folsom Mayor Glenn Fait regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1.
Is Mayor Fait precluded from snaking appointments to the
City of Folsom Redevelopment Agency Citizens Advisory Committee
based on his ownership of propery within the Redevelopment Area?
2.
Is Mayor Fait precluded from making appointments to the
City of Folsom Historical Area Committees based on his ownership
of property within the Historic District?
3.
Will the “public generally” exception affect the
determination of a potential conflict of interest with respect to
appointments to any of these committees?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government. Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
180Q0—18995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIPORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—042
Page
2
CONCLUSION
Absent a specific agreement with the appointees, it is not
foreseeable that the appointments will have a material financial
effect on Mayor Fait’s economic interests.
Therefore, Mayor Fait
is not preàluded from making appointments to the City of Folsom
Redevelopment Agency Citizens Advisory Committee and the
Historical Area Committees.
FACTS
,Mayor Fait was sworn in as a member of the Folsom City
Councjl on December 12,
1994,
and recently was elected by vote of
the city council to serve a one—year term as mayor commencing on
December 12,
1995.
As a councilmeiuber, Mayor Fait is also a
member of the Redevelopment Agency Boara of Directors.
At the time Mr. Fait was sworn in as a member of the city
council, he disclosed both in writing and orally on the record,
property holdings in the area encompassed within the Folsom
Redevelopment project Area.
Mr. Fait owns a home in which he and
his family reside as well as one residential rental property and
one commercial rental property in that area.
Each of Mr. Fait’s properties is also located within an area
defined by Folsom City Ordinance No.
17.52.020 as the “Historic
District.”
One of the responsibilities of the mayor is to appoint
committee members to the Redevelopment Agency Citizens Advisory
Committee (the “Redevelopment Committee”)
and the Historical
Committee/Historical Area Architectural Review Committee
(“Historical Area Committees”).
The Redevelopment Committee reviews and advises the
Redevelopment Agency, whose board consists of city councilmembers,
and the city council on certain enumerated issues.
The
Redevelopment Committee is only advisory to the city council and
the Redevelopment Agency,
and its actions do not obMgate either
body.
The Redevelopment Committee is the only citizens committee
formed to evaluate redevelopment related issues.
‘The Historical Area Committees do not act solely in an
advisory capacity to the city council; they review plans and
specifications.
A person dissatisfied with a decision of the
Historical Area Committees may appeal to the planning commission,
but• unless so appealed,
a. decision of the Historical Area
Committees would be final.
•
••
ANALYSIS
Section. 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
oficiai position to influence aqovernrnental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.


File No.
A—96—042
Page
3
A public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
a member of his or her immediate family, or on
any of the following:
(a)
Any business entitj :in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
•
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans 1y a commercial lending institution in
•
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a)—(d).
Mayor Fait has a residence and two rental properties,
presumably worth more than $1,000 each,
located in the Foisom
Redevelopment Project Area and the Historic District.
He may not
participate in any decision that would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on his real property
interests.
The decisions to appoint members of the Redevelopment
Committee and the Historical Area Committees are considered
governmental decisions.
Thus, the mayor must abstain from making
the appointments if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
appointments will have a material financial effect on his economic
interests.
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975>
1 FPPC Ops.
198
)
Absent a specific agreement between the mayor and the
appointees, it is n9t reasonably foreseeable that the appointment
of the committee members will affect Mayor Fait’s economic


File No.
A—96—042
Page 4
interests.
(Dorsey Advice Letter,
No. A-89-396; Benjamin Advice
Letter,
No.
A-86—148; Lofqren Advice Letter,
No.
A—86—307.)
2
You also asked if the public generally exception would apply
to this situation, however,
we do not need to reach this question.
Should you have a specific question about the public generally
exception in conneôtion with other decisions possibly affecting
Mayor Fait’s economic interests, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
•
By:
Liane RaTkdoiph
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak
2
Of course once the committee members are appointed, the mayor
may in no way attempt to influence the decisions of the agency as
tO specific decisions where the mayor would have a conflict of
interest.
(Dorsev Advice Letter, sura.)

QUESTION: 1.
Is Mayor Fait precluded from snaking appointments to the
City of Folsom Redevelopment Agency Citizens Advisory Committee
based on his ownership of propery within the Redevelopment Area?
2.
Is Mayor Fait precluded from making appointments to the
City of Folsom Historical Area Committees based on his ownership
of property within the Historic District?
3.
Will the “public generally” exception affect the
determination of a potential conflict of interest with respect to
appointments to any of these committees?
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
180Q0—18995.

(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—042

CONCLUSION: Absent a specific agreement with the appointees, it is not
foreseeable that the appointments will have a material financial
effect on Mayor Fait’s economic interests.
Therefore, Mayor Fait
is not preàluded from making appointments to the City of Folsom
Redevelopment Agency Citizens Advisory Committee and the
Historical Area Committees.
